verses.flow: Blessed is the man who does not walk in the counsel of the wicked, nor stand in the path of sinners, nor sit in the seat of scoffers.
verses.note: HEBREW: 'ashrey (blessed/happy) is a plural construct -- literally "blessednesses of the man." The three verbs -- walk, stand, sit -- show a downward progression into sin: casual contact, then lingering, then settled identification. The three nouns likewise intensify: counsel (advice), path (habitual way), seat (permanent position). The wicked, sinners, and scoffers represent increasing degrees of hostility toward God.

words.g: and + in/with a precept or statute

verses.flow: But his delight is in the law of the LORD, and on His law he meditates day and night.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Meditates" (yehgeh) comes from hagah, meaning to mutter, murmur, or growl -- it implies audible, repetitive recitation. Ancient Torah study was oral, not silent reading. "Day and night" echoes the command given to Joshua (Josh. 1:8). The Torah (law/instruction) is the entire revelation of God, not merely legal code.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And he shall be like a tree planted by streams of water, which yields its fruit in its season, and its leaf does not wither; and in all that he does, he prospers.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The tree image draws from Jer. 17:7-8 and Ezek. 47:12. The tree planted (shatul -- deliberately transplanted) by water channels is sustained by a source outside itself. The righteous man's fruitfulness is not self-generated but comes from rootedness in God's word. "In its season" implies patience and divine timing.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: The wicked are not so, but they are like chaff which the wind drives away.
Therefore the wicked will not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the assembly of the righteous.
For the LORD knows the way of the righteous, but the way of the wicked will perish.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Knows" (yodea') is not mere intellectual awareness but intimate, relational knowledge -- the same verb used for covenant relationship (Gen. 4:1, Amos 3:2). The LORD "knows" the way of the righteous in the sense of watching over, approving, and sustaining it. The way of the wicked "perishes" (to'veid) -- it disintegrates, comes to nothing.

verses.flow: Why do the nations rage, and the peoples meditate on a vain thing?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Rage" (ragshu) describes the tumultuous plotting of nations. "Meditate" (yehgu) is the same verb used positively in Ps. 1:2 -- there, meditating on Torah; here, meditating on rebellion. The contrast is deliberate: the blessed man meditates on God's law, the nations meditate on a vain thing (riq -- emptiness). MESSIANIC: Acts 4:25-26 quotes this verse directly, applying it to the conspiracy against Jesus.

verses.flow: The kings of the earth take their stand, and the rulers take counsel together, against the LORD and against His Anointed, saying,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Anointed" (meshicho) = His Messiah (mashiach). The kings rebel against both YHWH and His Anointed -- the two are inseparable. MESSIANIC: This is the foundational messianic text. Acts 4:26-27 identifies Herod, Pilate, the Gentiles, and the peoples of Israel as the kings and rulers who gathered against God's Anointed, Jesus.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil cohortative 1st common plur

verses.flow: "Let us tear their bonds apart, and cast their cords from us."
He who sits in the heavens laughs; the Lord mocks them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God's response to human rebellion is laughter (yischaq) -- not nervousness but sovereign derision. The verb implies the utter absurdity of creatures rebelling against the Creator. "He who sits in the heavens" (yoshev bashamayim) -- God is seated, unmoved, enthroned. The contrast between the frantic activity of verse 1-3 and God's serene sovereignty here is devastating.

words.g: and + in/with a burning of anger

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Then He speaks to them in His anger, and in His burning wrath He terrifies them:

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: "But as for Me, I have installed My King upon Zion, My holy mountain."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Installed" (nasakhti) can also mean "poured out" (as a libation), suggesting consecration. Zion (tsiyon) is God's chosen mountain, the seat of His earthly rule. God's response to rebellion is not defensive but assertive: He installs His King anyway. MESSIANIC: Jesus is the King installed on Zion -- rejected by men, enthroned by God (Acts 2:36).

words.m: verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: I will tell of the decree: the LORD said to Me, "You are My Son; today I have begotten You.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "You are My Son; today I have begotten You" -- the most quoted OT verse in the NT. Acts 13:33 applies it to the resurrection of Jesus. Heb. 1:5 uses it to establish Christ's superiority over angels. Heb. 5:5 connects it to Christ's high priesthood. The "decree" (choq) is an eternal divine ordinance. "Today I have begotten You" (ylidtikha) does not imply origin in time but formal installation -- the language of royal adoption in the ancient Near East. The divine council context: YHWH declares His King before the heavenly assembly.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal cohortative 1st common sing

verses.flow: Ask of Me, and I will give the nations as Your inheritance, and the ends of the earth as Your possession.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb piel imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: You shall break them with a rod of iron; You shall shatter them like a potter's vessel."
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "Rod of iron" (shevet barzel) -- Rev. 2:27, 12:5, and 19:15 all apply this to Christ's eschatological rule. The "potter's vessel" image conveys the ease with which God's Anointed will shatter all opposition. This is not negotiated peace but absolute victory.

verses.flow: Now therefore, O kings, be wise; be warned, O judges of the earth.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Serve the LORD with fear, and rejoice with trembling.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Kiss the Son, lest He be angry and you perish in the way, for His wrath may soon be kindled. Blessed are all who take refuge in Him.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Kiss the Son" (nashqu-var) -- bar is the Aramaic word for "son," unusual in a Hebrew psalm. Some scholars read it as "purity" (bar = pure), yielding "kiss purely" or "do homage with purity," but the messianic reading is ancient and supported by the context (v.7 "You are My Son"). The kiss is an act of submission and homage (cf. 1 Sam. 10:1). "Blessed are all who take refuge in Him" -- note that refuge is taken in the Son, an extraordinary statement if the Son is merely human. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: Taking refuge in the Son is taking refuge in God -- an implicit claim to the Son's divinity.

verses.flow: A Psalm of David, when he fled from Absalom his son.
verses.note: HEBREW: The superscription connects this psalm to David's flight from Absalom (2 Sam. 15-18). "Selah" (selah) appears three times in this psalm. Its meaning is uncertain -- possibly a musical interlude, a liturgical instruction, or a marker for meditation. The LXX renders it diapsalma (interlude).

verses.flow: O LORD, how many are my adversaries! Many are rising up against me.

verses.flow: But You, O LORD, are a shield about me, my glory, and the One who lifts my head.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shield about me" (magen ba'adi) -- magen is a small defensive shield. "About me" (ba'adi) means surrounding, encircling -- God is not merely in front of David but all around him. "The One who lifts my head" (merim roshi) -- when surrounded by enemies, David's head is bowed in shame; God lifts it in restored dignity.

verses.flow: Arise, O LORD! Save me, O my God! For You have struck all my enemies on the cheek; You have shattered the teeth of the wicked.
Salvation belongs to the LORD; Your blessing be upon Your people. Selah.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Salvation belongs to the LORD" (la-YHWH hayeshu'ah) -- salvation is not a human achievement but a divine prerogative. This is the theological summary of the psalm. The blessing extends to "Your people" ('ammekha) -- David's personal deliverance is connected to corporate blessing.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Tremble, and do not sin; meditate in your heart upon your bed, and be still. Selah.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tremble" (rigzu) can also mean "be agitated" or "be angry" -- the sense is emotional disturbance. Paul quotes the LXX in Eph. 4:26: "Be angry and do not sin." The Hebrew pairs inner turmoil with stillness (dommu) -- feel the emotion but bring it to God on your bed, in silence, rather than acting on it sinfully.

verses.note: HEBREW: "Lift up the light of Your face" (nesah 'aleinu 'or panekha) echoes the Aaronic blessing of Num. 6:25-26. The "light of God's face" is His favor, approval, and presence. David's joy exceeds the material prosperity of abundant harvest.

verses.flow: You have put gladness in my heart, more than when their grain and new wine abound.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 1st common sing

verses.note: HEBREW: "In the morning I will order my prayer" (boqer 'e'erokh lekha) -- 'arokh means to arrange or set in order, as a priest arranges wood on the altar (Lev. 1:7) or a legal case before a judge. Prayer is deliberate, structured, and sacrificial. "Eagerly watch" ('atsappeh) -- like a watchman scanning the horizon.

verses.flow: For there is nothing reliable in their mouth; their inward part is destruction; their throat is an open grave; they flatter with their tongue.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Their throat is an open grave" -- Paul quotes this verse in Rom. 3:13 as part of his catena of OT texts proving universal human sinfulness (Rom. 3:10-18). The internal corruption of the wicked manifests through their speech.

verses.flow: Return, O LORD, rescue my soul; save me because of Your lovingkindness.

verses.flow: For in death there is no remembrance of You; in Sheol who will give You thanks?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "In death there is no remembrance of You; in Sheol who will give You thanks?" -- This reflects the OT understanding of Sheol as a shadowy, diminished existence where praise ceases (cf. Ps. 30:9, 88:10-12, 115:17, Isa. 38:18). It does not deny an afterlife but expresses the anguish of separation from the worshiping community and the felt presence of God.

verses.flow: I am weary with my sighing; every night I flood my bed with tears; I drench my couch with my weeping.
My eye has wasted away because of grief; it has grown old because of all my adversaries.
Depart from me, all you workers of iniquity, for the LORD has heard the voice of my weeping.
The LORD has heard my supplication; the LORD receives my prayer.

verses.flow: All my enemies will be ashamed and greatly dismayed; they will turn back, they will suddenly be put to shame.
A Shiggaion of David, which he sang to the LORD concerning the words of Cush, a Benjaminite.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shiggaion" (shiggayon) -- an uncertain musical term, possibly related to shagah (to wander, to reel), suggesting an erratic, impassioned style. Only here and Hab. 3:1. "Cush, a Benjaminite" -- this figure is unknown outside this superscription; possibly a supporter of Saul.

verses.note: HEBREW: "A righteous judge" (shophet tsaddiq) -- God judges continuously, not merely at a future assize. "Indignant every day" (zo'em bekhol yom) -- God's wrath is a present, ongoing moral response to evil, not an occasional outburst.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: O LORD, our Lord, how majestic is Your name in all the earth, who have displayed Your splendor above the heavens!
verses.note: HEBREW: "LORD" (YHWH) and "Lord" ('Adonenu -- our Lord) are distinct. YHWH is the covenant name; 'Adon is the title of sovereign authority. "How majestic" (mah-'addir) -- 'addir connotes splendor, magnificence, and noble power. The name of God fills all the earth and surpasses the heavens.

verses.flow: From the mouth of infants and nursing babes You have established strength because of Your adversaries, to make the enemy and the revengeful cease.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: Jesus quotes this verse in Matt. 21:16 when children cry "Hosanna to the Son of David" in the temple and the chief priests are indignant. God establishes His praise from the weakest vessels to confound the strong. "Strength" ('oz) in the Hebrew; the LXX reads "praise" (ainon), which Jesus follows.

verses.flow: When I consider Your heavens, the work of Your fingers, the moon and the stars, which You have set in place --

words.m: verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: what is man that You are mindful of him, and the son of man that You care for him?
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "What is man" (mah-'enosh) -- 'enosh emphasizes human frailty and mortality. "The son of man" (ben-'adam) is a parallel term for humanity. Heb. 2:6-8 applies this entire passage to Christ's incarnation: He was made "a little lower" than the angels (following the LXX reading of elohim as angels) in order to be crowned with glory through His suffering and death.

verses.flow: Yet You have made him a little lower than God, and You crown him with glory and majesty!
verses.note: HEBREW: "A little lower than God" (me'at me'elohim) -- elohim here could mean "God," "gods," or "heavenly beings" (divine council members). The LXX translated it as "angels" (angelous), which Heb. 2:7 follows. The Hebrew is bolder: humanity is made just below the divine tier. "Crown him with glory and majesty" (kavod wehadar te'attereihu) -- royal language applied to humanity as God's vice-regent over creation. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: Christ is the true Man who fulfills this role perfectly -- crowned with glory after His humiliation (Heb. 2:9).

verses.note: MESSIANIC: "You have put all things under his feet" -- 1 Cor. 15:27 and Eph. 1:22 apply this directly to Christ's exaltation and universal lordship. What Adam lost through the fall, Christ reclaims through resurrection.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "All his thoughts are, 'There is no God'" -- the Hebrew is more literally "God is not in all his thoughts/schemes." This is practical atheism: not a philosophical denial but a functional exclusion of God from all considerations. The wicked does not think through his plans with any reference to God's existence, character, or judgment.

The LORD is in His holy temple; the LORD's throne is in heaven. His eyes behold, His eyelids test the sons of men.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The LORD is in His holy temple; the LORD's throne is in heaven" -- the earthly temple and heavenly throne are connected. God's presence in the temple is real but His sovereignty extends to heaven. "His eyes behold, His eyelids test" -- anthropomorphic language for divine omniscience. God sees and evaluates everything, particularly the hearts of men.

verses.flow: The LORD tests the righteous and the wicked, and the one who loves violence His soul hates.

verses.flow: Save, O LORD, for the godly man ceases to be, for the faithful disappear from among the sons of men.
They speak falsehood to one another; with flattering lips and with a double heart they speak.
May the LORD cut off all flattering lips, the tongue that speaks great things;
who have said, "With our tongue we will prevail; our lips are our own; who is lord over us?"
"Because of the devastation of the afflicted, because of the groaning of the needy, now I will arise," says the LORD; "I will set him in the safety for which he longs."
The words of the LORD are pure words; as silver refined in a furnace on the earth, purified seven times.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Pure words" ('amarot tehorot) -- 'amarah (saying/utterance) is a feminine form emphasizing the spoken quality of God's word. "Silver refined in a furnace on the earth, purified seven times" -- the most rigorous purification process known. Seven is the number of completeness. God's words contain no dross, no admixture of falsehood -- in direct contrast to the lying speech of men in vv.2-5. This is one of the strongest statements on the purity and reliability of Scripture.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: How long, O LORD? Will You forget me forever? How long will You hide Your face from me?
verses.note: HEBREW: Four times "how long" ('ad-'anah) -- the fourfold repetition intensifies the anguish. The psalmist feels forgotten (v.2a), hidden from (v.2b), internally tormented (v.3a), and publicly defeated (v.3b). The structure moves from God's action (or inaction) to the psalmist's internal state to the external enemy -- the full scope of suffering.

verses.flow: How long shall I take counsel in my soul, having sorrow in my heart all the day? How long will my enemy be exalted over me?

But I have trusted in Your lovingkindness; my heart shall rejoice in Your salvation. I will sing to the LORD, because He has dealt bountifully with me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The psalm moves from lament (vv.2-3) to petition (vv.4-5) to trust (v.6) in six short verses -- one of the most compressed examples of the lament-to-praise trajectory in the Psalter. "I have trusted" (batachti) is a perfect tense -- the decision to trust is already made even before deliverance comes. Faith precedes experience.

verses.flow: For the choir director. Of David. The fool has said in his heart, "There is no God." They are corrupt, they have done abominable deeds; there is no one who does good.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Fool" (naval) is not intellectual stupidity but moral perversity -- the naval is one who disregards God and acts as though there will be no reckoning (cf. Nabal in 1 Sam. 25). "There is no God" ('ein 'Elohim) -- not philosophical atheism (which barely existed in the ancient world) but practical denial: God does not act, does not judge, does not matter. THEOLOGICAL: Paul quotes vv.1-3 in Rom. 3:10-12 to prove the universal sinfulness of humanity.

verses.flow: The LORD has looked down from heaven upon the sons of men to see if there are any who understand, who seek after God.

verses.flow: A Psalm of David. O LORD, who may sojourn in Your tent? Who may dwell on Your holy hill?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Who may sojourn in Your tent? Who may dwell on Your holy hill?" -- the question is about access to God's presence, not merely entering the temple courts. "Sojourn" (yagur) implies temporary dwelling; "dwell" (yishkon) implies permanent residence. The answer in vv.2-5 describes not cultic requirements but ethical character -- integrity, truth-telling, loyalty, justice. This is not salvation by works but a description of the character that genuine faith produces.

verses.flow: He who walks with integrity and works righteousness, and speaks truth in his heart.
He does not slander with his tongue, nor does evil to his neighbor, nor takes up a reproach against his friend.
In whose eyes a reprobate is despised, but who honors those who fear the LORD; he swears to his own hurt and does not change.
He does not put out his money at interest, nor does he take a bribe against the innocent. He who does these things shall never be shaken.

verses.note: MESSIANIC: Peter quotes vv.8-11 in his Pentecost sermon (Acts 2:25-28) as the words of David speaking prophetically of Christ. "I have set the LORD always before me" -- Christ's unbroken communion with the Father throughout His earthly life. "He is at my right hand, I shall not be shaken" -- even in the face of the cross.

verses.flow: For You will not abandon my soul to Sheol; nor will You allow Your Holy One to see decay.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "You will not abandon my soul to Sheol; nor will You allow Your Holy One to see decay" (lo'-tittein chasidkha lir'ot shachat). This is the climax of the psalm and a direct prophecy of bodily resurrection. Peter argues in Acts 2:27-31 that David could not have been speaking of himself (since David died and his tomb was still present), but spoke prophetically of Christ, whose body did not see decay because God raised Him. Paul makes the same argument in Acts 13:35-37. "Holy One" (chasid) = the faithful/devoted one; "decay" (shachat) = pit/corruption/decomposition.

verses.note: MESSIANIC: "The path of life... fullness of joy... pleasures forevermore" -- this is resurrection language. David sees beyond death to eternal, embodied joy in God's presence. Peter applies this to the risen Christ (Acts 2:28). The resurrection of Jesus is the "path of life" opened for all who trust in Him.

verses.note: MESSIANIC: "I shall behold Your face in righteousness; I will be satisfied with Your likeness when I awake" ('ani betsedek 'echezeh fanekha 'esbe'ah behaqits temunathekha). This is one of the earliest expressions of resurrection hope in the Psalter. "When I awake" -- David anticipates waking from death to see God's face and be fully satisfied. "Your likeness" (temunah) -- the beatific vision, seeing God as He is (cf. 1 John 3:2).

verses.note: HEBREW: This psalm has a parallel in 2 Sam. 22, with minor variations. It is one of the longest psalms and one of the most important royal psalms. The superscription identifies it as David's song after all his deliverances. "Servant of the LORD" ('eved YHWH) is a title of honor -- Moses (Deut. 34:5), Joshua (Josh. 24:29), and the Suffering Servant (Isa. 52:13) share this designation.

verses.flow: Then the earth shook and quaked; and the foundations of the mountains were trembling and were shaken, because He was angry.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The theophany in vv.8-16 is one of the most dramatic in the OT. Earth-shaking, smoke from God's nostrils, devouring fire, descent on thick darkness -- this is Sinai language (Exod. 19:16-18) applied to personal deliverance. God's salvation is cosmic in scope. The natural world convulses when its Creator acts to save.

verses.flow: Therefore I will give thanks to You among the nations, O LORD, and I will sing praises to Your name.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "Therefore I will give thanks to You among the nations, O LORD" -- Paul quotes this verse in Rom. 15:9 as evidence that God's plan always included the Gentiles. David's praise among the nations foreshadows Christ's praise among all peoples.

verses.flow: He gives great salvation to His king, and shows lovingkindness to His anointed, to David and his seed forever.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "He shows lovingkindness to His anointed (limshicho), to David and his seed forever" -- the promise extends beyond David to his dynasty and ultimately to the Messiah. "Forever" ('ad-'olam) can only be fulfilled by an eternal King -- Jesus, the Son of David (Matt. 1:1, Luke 1:32-33).

verses.flow: For the choir director. A Psalm of David.
The heavens are telling of the glory of God; and the expanse is declaring the work of His hands.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The heavens are telling" (hashamayim mesapperim) -- the participle indicates continuous action: the heavens never stop declaring. This is general revelation (Rom. 1:19-20). "The glory of God" (kevod-'El) -- kavod means weight, substance, splendor. The cosmos is a perpetual sermon on God's attributes.

verses.flow: Day to day pours forth speech, and night to night reveals knowledge.
There is no speech, nor are there words; their voice is not heard.
Their line has gone out through all the earth, and their utterances to the end of the world. In them He has placed a tent for the sun,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Their line" (qawwam) -- the MT reads qav (line, measuring cord), suggesting the heavens' testimony stretches like a boundary marker across all the earth. The LXX reads phthongos (sound, voice), which Paul follows in Rom. 10:18 to argue that the gospel has gone out to all nations.

verses.flow: which is as a bridegroom coming out of his chamber; it rejoices as a strong man to run his course.
Its rising is from one end of the heavens, and its circuit to the other end of them; and there is nothing hidden from its heat.
The law of the LORD is perfect, restoring the soul; the testimony of the LORD is sure, making wise the simple.
verses.note: HEBREW: Six terms for God's revelation appear in vv.8-10, each with a descriptor and a result: law (torah) - perfect - restoring; testimony ('edut) - sure - making wise; precepts (piqqudim) - right - rejoicing; commandment (mitsvah) - pure - enlightening; fear (yir'ah) - clean - enduring; judgments (mishpatim) - true - righteous. This is the most comprehensive celebration of Torah in the Psalter outside Psalm 119.

verses.flow: The precepts of the LORD are right, rejoicing the heart; the commandment of the LORD is pure, enlightening the eyes.
The fear of the LORD is clean, enduring forever; the judgments of the LORD are true; they are righteous altogether.

"Rock" (tsur) and "redeemer" (go'el) -- God is both immovable foundation and active kinsman-redeemer.

The "saving strength of His right hand" (bigvurot yesha' yemino) -- God's right hand is the hand of power and authority (cf. Ps. 110:1).

verses.flow: Some boast in chariots and some in horses, but we will boast in the name of the LORD our God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Some boast in chariots and some in horses, but we will boast in the name of the LORD our God" -- this is the fundamental contrast between trust in military technology and trust in God. Israel was forbidden from multiplying horses (Deut. 17:16). The "name" (shem) of YHWH is not merely a label but the revelation of His character and power.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb hithpolel sequential imperfect 1st common plur

verses.flow: They have bowed down and fallen, but we have risen and stood upright.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Save, O LORD; may the King answer us in the day we call.
For the choir director. A Psalm of David.
O LORD, in Your strength the king will be glad, and in Your salvation how greatly he rejoices!
You have given him his heart's desire, and You have not withheld the request of his lips. Selah.

words.m: verb piel imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: For You meet him with the blessings of good things; You set a crown of fine gold on his head.
He asked life of You, You gave it to him, length of days forever and ever.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "He asked life of You, You gave it to him, length of days forever and ever" ('orekh yamim 'olam wa'ed) -- this exceeds anything a mortal king could receive. David lived long, but not "forever and ever." This points to the eternal life of the Messianic King -- Christ, who asked the Father for life (John 17:5) and received it through resurrection, now living forever (Rev. 1:18).

verses.flow: His glory is great through Your salvation; splendor and majesty You place upon him.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb piel imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: For You make him most blessed forever; You make him joyful with gladness in Your presence.
For the king trusts in the LORD, and through the lovingkindness of the Most High he will not be shaken.
Your hand will find out all your enemies; your right hand will find out those who hate you.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: You will make them as a fiery oven in the time of your anger; the LORD will swallow them up in His wrath, and fire will devour them.
Their offspring you will destroy from the earth, and their seed from among the sons of men.
Though they intended evil against you and devised a plot, they will not succeed.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: For you will make them turn their back; you will aim with your bowstrings at their faces.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb piel cohortative 1st common plur

verses.flow: Be exalted, O LORD, in Your strength; we will sing and praise Your power.
For the choir director; upon Aijeleth Hashshahar. A Psalm of David.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Aijeleth Hashshahar" ('ayyelet hashachar) = "Doe of the Dawn" -- possibly a tune name. Some see symbolic significance: the dawn follows the darkest night, as resurrection follows the cross.

words.m: verb qal perfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: My God, my God, why have You forsaken me? Far from my salvation are the words of my groaning.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "My God, my God, why have You forsaken me?" ('Eli 'Eli lamah 'azavtani) -- Jesus cried these exact words from the cross (Matt. 27:46, Mark 15:34), in Aramaic: "Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani." By quoting the first verse of this psalm, Jesus identifies His entire passion with its full narrative arc -- from abandonment (vv.1-21) to vindication and universal worship (vv.22-31). This is not mere despair but prophetic fulfillment. The cry is real suffering (genuine forsakenness as the sin-bearer, 2 Cor. 5:21) within the framework of ultimate trust.

verses.flow: O my God, I cry by day, but You do not answer; and by night, but I have no rest.
Yet You are holy, O You who are enthroned upon the praises of Israel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "You are holy, O You who are enthroned upon the praises of Israel" -- even in the depths of forsakenness, the sufferer affirms God's holiness. God's character is not questioned, only His silence. The paradox of the cross: the Holy God is silent while His Holy One suffers.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: In You our fathers trusted; they trusted, and You delivered them.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: To You they cried out and were delivered; in You they trusted and were not put to shame.
But I am a worm and not a man, a reproach of men and despised by the people.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "I am a worm and not a man" (tola'at welo'-'ish) -- tola'at is specifically the crimson worm (coccus ilicis), whose crushed body produces scarlet dye. The despised sufferer is crushed to bring forth something precious. "Reproach of men and despised by the people" -- Isa. 53:3: "despised and rejected by men."

verses.flow: All who see me mock me; they make mouths at me, they wag the head, saying,
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "All who see me mock me; they make mouths at me, they wag the head" -- Matt. 27:39: "Those passing by were hurling abuse at Him, wagging their heads." The correspondence is exact: the physical gestures of mockery at the cross were prophesied a thousand years earlier.

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: "He committed himself to the LORD; let Him deliver him; let Him rescue him, because He delights in him."
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "He committed himself to the LORD; let Him deliver him" -- Matt. 27:43: "He trusts in God; let God rescue Him now, if He delights in Him; for He said, 'I am the Son of God.'" The chief priests, scribes, and elders unknowingly spoke the words of this psalm while mocking Jesus.

verses.flow: Yet You are He who brought me forth from the womb; You made me trust when upon my mother's breasts.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "You brought me forth from the womb" -- even in the agony of abandonment, the sufferer recalls God's faithfulness from birth. God's care is woven into the sufferer's entire life history. For Christ, this recalls the incarnation itself -- God brought His Son into the world through Mary's womb (Gal. 4:4).

verses.flow: Upon You I was cast from birth; from my mother's womb You have been my God.
Be not far from me, for trouble is near; for there is none to help.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix
verb piel perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: Many bulls have surrounded me; strong bulls of Bashan have encircled me.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "Strong bulls of Bashan" ('abbirey Vashan) -- Bashan was known for its powerful cattle (Amos 4:1, Ezek. 39:18). These represent the powerful enemies surrounding the crucified Christ: the Roman soldiers, the religious leaders, the hostile crowd.

words.g: to pluck off or pull to pieces

verses.flow: They open wide their mouth at me, as a ravening and a roaring lion.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "They open wide their mouth at me, as a ravening and a roaring lion" -- the enemies anticipate devouring the sufferer. At the cross, the combined forces of religious and political power opened their jaws against the Lamb of God.

words.m: conjunction + verb hithpael perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint; my heart is like wax; it is melted within me.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint; my heart is like wax; it is melted within me" -- a precise description of crucifixion. The body's weight, suspended by nails, pulls joints apart. Extreme dehydration and shock cause the heart to fail. The pericardial sac fills with fluid (cf. John 19:34 -- "blood and water came out").

words.m: verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: My strength is dried up like a potsherd, and my tongue cleaves to my jaws; and You lay me in the dust of death.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "My strength is dried up like a potsherd, and my tongue cleaves to my jaws" -- extreme dehydration. John 19:28: "Jesus said, 'I am thirsty.'" "You lay me in the dust of death" -- the sufferer attributes even death to God's sovereign will. Christ's death was not an accident but the Father's plan (Acts 2:23, Isa. 53:10).

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix
verb hiphil perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: For dogs have surrounded me; a band of evildoers has encompassed me; they pierced my hands and my feet.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "They pierced my hands and my feet" -- the Hebrew is disputed. The MT reads ka'ari ("like a lion"), but the LXX, Syriac, and several Hebrew manuscripts (including a Dead Sea Scroll fragment, 5/6HevPs) read ka'aru ("they pierced/dug"). The difference is a single letter: yod vs. vav. The piercing reading aligns with crucifixion (John 20:25-27, where Thomas sees the nail marks). Even the MT reading ("like a lion [at] my hands and feet") implies mauling that is functionally equivalent to piercing.

verses.flow: I can count all my bones. They look, they stare at me.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "I can count all my bones" -- the victim is stretched and emaciated, bones protruding and visible. Crucifixion was designed for maximum exposure and humiliation. "They look, they stare at me" -- the spectacle of public execution. Luke 23:35: "And the people stood by, watching."

verses.flow: They divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "They divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots" -- John 19:23-24 records the precise fulfillment: the soldiers divided Jesus' garments into four parts and cast lots for the seamless tunic. John explicitly says "that the Scripture might be fulfilled" and quotes this verse. The detail is so specific that it could not have been invented after the fact to match the psalm.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: But You, O LORD, be not far off; O You my help, hasten to my assistance.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "But You, O LORD, be not far off" -- the turning point of the psalm. After the nadir of suffering (vv.1-19), the sufferer turns again to God in desperate hope. The cry of forsakenness gives way to the plea for nearness. At the cross, the darkness lifts (Matt. 27:45-50).

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Deliver my soul from the sword, my only life from the power of the dog.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Save me from the mouth of the lion; and from the horns of the wild oxen You answer me.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "Save me from the mouth of the lion; and from the horns of the wild oxen You answer me" -- the verb shifts: "You answer me" ('anitani). God responds. This is the hinge of the psalm: from this point forward, everything is praise, vindication, and triumph. The resurrection is the answer. Heb. 5:7: "He was heard because of His reverent submission."

words.m: verb piel cohortative 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: I will tell of Your name to my brethren; in the midst of the assembly I will praise You.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "I will tell of Your name to my brethren; in the midst of the assembly I will praise You" -- Heb. 2:12 quotes this verse and applies it directly to Christ, who calls believers His "brethren." After the resurrection, Christ declares God's name to His brothers (John 20:17). The suffering of vv.1-21 leads to the worship of vv.22-31.

words.m: verb piel imperative 2nd masc plur + suffix
verb piel imperative 2nd masc plur + suffix
conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: You who fear the LORD, praise Him; all you seed of Jacob, glorify Him, and stand in awe of Him, all you seed of Israel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The call to praise expands outward: "you who fear the LORD" -> "all you seed of Jacob" -> "all you seed of Israel." The resurrection generates concentric circles of worship that will ultimately encompass the whole earth (vv.27-28).

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb piel infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: For He has not despised nor abhorred the affliction of the afflicted; nor has He hidden His face from him; but when he cried to Him for help, He heard.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "He has not despised nor abhorred the affliction of the afflicted" -- though it seemed like God had forsaken the sufferer (v.1), in reality He had not turned away. The resurrection proves that God heard the cry of the cross. God did not despise the suffering -- He vindicated it.

verses.flow: From You comes my praise in the great assembly; I shall pay my vows before those who fear Him.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: The afflicted will eat and be satisfied; those who seek Him will praise the LORD. Let your heart live forever!
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "The afflicted will eat and be satisfied" -- this is messianic banquet language (Isa. 25:6-8, Matt. 8:11, Rev. 19:9). The resurrection of Christ inaugurates the feast of salvation. "Let your heart live forever" -- the promise of eternal life flowing from the sufferer's vindication.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur
conjunction + verb hishtaphel imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: All the ends of the earth will remember and turn to the LORD, and all the families of the nations will worship before You.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "All the ends of the earth will remember and turn to the LORD, and all the families of the nations will worship before You" -- universal worship. The crucified and risen Messiah draws all peoples to Himself (John 12:32). This is the Great Commission in seed form.

verses.flow: For the kingdom is the LORD's, and He rules over the nations.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "For the kingdom is the LORD's, and He rules over the nations" -- the psalm that began with forsakenness ends with universal sovereignty. The cross is not defeat but the means by which God establishes His kingdom over all nations.

words.m: conjunction + verb hishtaphel sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: All the prosperous of the earth will eat and worship; all those who go down to the dust will bow before Him, even he who cannot keep his soul alive.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: A seed will serve Him; it will be told of the Lord to the coming generation.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "A seed will serve Him; it will be told of the Lord to the coming generation" -- the proclamation extends across time as well as space. Every generation will hear of what God did through the suffering and resurrection of His Anointed.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: They will come and will declare His righteousness to a people yet to be born, that He has done it.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "They will come and will declare His righteousness to a people yet to be born, that He has done it" -- the final words: ki 'asah, "that He has done it" or "for He has accomplished it." Compare John 19:30: tetelestai, "It is finished." The work of salvation is complete. The psalm ends not with a prayer but with a declaration: God has acted, and the results will echo through all generations to peoples not yet born.

verses.flow: A Psalm of David. The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The LORD is my shepherd" (YHWH ro'i) -- the most intimate of all divine titles. The shepherd metaphor implies ownership, provision, protection, guidance, and intimate knowledge of each sheep. "I shall not want" (lo' 'echsar) -- not "I will never desire" but "I will lack nothing I truly need." CHRIST TYPOLOGY: Jesus declares "I am the Good Shepherd" (John 10:11), identifying Himself as the YHWH of Psalm 23. The Good Shepherd "lays down His life for the sheep" -- this shepherd psalm must be read through the lens of the crucifixion psalm (Ps. 22) that precedes it.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: He makes me lie down in green pastures; He leads me beside still waters.
verses.note: HEBREW: "He makes me lie down" (yarbitseni) -- sheep will not lie down unless they are free from fear, friction, pests, and hunger. The shepherd creates the conditions for rest. "Still waters" (mey menuchot) -- literally "waters of resting places." Sheep fear rushing water; the shepherd leads to calm pools.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: He restores my soul; He guides me in the paths of righteousness for His name's sake.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Restores my soul" (nafshi yeshovev) -- yeshovev means to bring back, turn back, or refresh. The wandering sheep is retrieved and revived. "Paths of righteousness" (ma'agley-tsedeq) -- ma'agal means a track or wagon rut, a well-worn path. "For His name's sake" -- God's motivation is His own glory and faithfulness to His covenant character.

words.g: in/with a gorge (from its lofty sides

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for You are with me; Your rod and Your staff, they comfort me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Valley of the shadow of death" (gey' tsalmavet) -- tsalmavet may be a compound (tsel + mavet = shadow of death) or a superlative (tsalmut = deep darkness). Either way, it is the darkest possible place. "Your rod and Your staff" -- the rod (shevet) was a weapon against predators; the staff (mish'enet) was a walking stick used to guide sheep. Together they represent protection and direction. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: Christ walked through the valley of death itself (Ps. 22) and emerged as the resurrected Shepherd (Heb. 13:20).

verses.flow: You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies; You have anointed my head with oil; my cup overflows.
verses.note: HEBREW: "You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies" -- the metaphor shifts from pastoral to royal/covenantal. The table is a feast, and the enemies must watch the honored guest dine. "Anointed my head with oil" (dishanta vashemen roshi) -- anointing signified honor, joy, and consecration. "My cup overflows" (kosi revayah) -- superabundant provision.

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Surely goodness and lovingkindness will follow me all the days of my life, and I will dwell in the house of the LORD forever.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Goodness and lovingkindness" (tov wachesed) -- chesed is covenant loyalty, God's unfailing love. "Will follow me" (yirdefuni) -- the verb radaf normally means "pursue" or "chase," often used of enemies in pursuit. Here it is God's goodness that pursues -- relentlessly, inescapably. "I will dwell in the house of the LORD forever" (weshavti beveit-YHWH le'orekh yamim) -- the psalm ends in the temple, in God's presence, for the length of days. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: The sheep who follows the Good Shepherd arrives at the Father's house (John 14:2-3).

verses.flow: A Psalm of David. The earth is the LORD's, and all it contains, the world, and those who dwell in it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The earth is the LORD's, and all it contains" -- Paul quotes this in 1 Cor. 10:26 to settle a question about food offered to idols. God's ownership of all creation is the foundation for both worship and ethics.

words.g: a sea or large body of water

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
verb polel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: For He has founded it upon the seas, and established it upon the rivers.

words.g: in/with a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: Who may ascend into the hill of the LORD? And who may stand in His holy place?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Who may ascend into the hill of the LORD?" -- like Psalm 15, this asks who has access to God's presence. The "hill of the LORD" (har-YHWH) is Mount Zion, the temple mount. The question presumes that not everyone can approach.

verses.flow: He who has clean hands and a pure heart, who has not lifted up his soul to falsehood and has not sworn deceitfully.

words.g: from gods in the ordinary sense

verses.flow: He shall receive a blessing from the LORD, and righteousness from the God of his salvation.
This is the generation of those who seek Him, who seek Your face -- even Jacob. Selah.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal imperative 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Lift up your heads, O gates, and be lifted up, O ancient doors, that the King of glory may come in!
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "Lift up your heads, O gates, and be lifted up, O ancient doors, that the King of glory may come in!" -- this dramatic liturgy has been applied to three moments: (1) David bringing the Ark to Jerusalem (2 Sam. 6), (2) Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem, and (3) Christ's ascension into the heavenly temple (cf. Heb. 9:24). The "ancient doors" (pithchey 'olam) are the eternal gates of heaven. The King of glory demands entry -- and the gates must open for their Lord.

verses.flow: Who is the King of glory? The LORD strong and mighty, the LORD mighty in battle.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Lift up your heads, O gates, and lift them up, O ancient doors, that the King of glory may come in!
Who is this King of glory? The LORD of hosts, He is the King of glory. Selah.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The LORD of hosts" (YHWH tseva'ot) -- the LORD of armies, both heavenly and earthly. This divine title appears over 260 times in the OT and emphasizes God's sovereign command over all powers. "He is the King of glory" -- the final, definitive answer. The psalm ends with the identification complete: the King of glory is YHWH Himself, the commander of all heavenly and earthly hosts. Selah.

verses.flow: Of David. To You, O LORD, I lift up my soul.
verses.note: HEBREW: This is an acrostic psalm -- each verse begins with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet (aleph through tav), with some irregularities. Acrostic structure served as a memory aid and symbolized completeness (A to Z = everything). "Of David" is attached to v.1 in the Hebrew.

verses.flow: O my God, in You I trust; do not let me be ashamed; do not let my enemies exult over me.
Indeed, none of those who wait for You will be ashamed; those who deal treacherously without cause will be ashamed.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb piel imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Make me know Your ways, O LORD; teach me Your paths.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb piel imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Lead me in Your truth and teach me, for You are the God of my salvation; for You I wait all the day.
Remember, O LORD, Your compassion and Your lovingkindnesses, for they have been from of old.
Do not remember the sins of my youth or my transgressions; according to Your lovingkindness remember me, for Your goodness' sake, O LORD.
Good and upright is the LORD; therefore He instructs sinners in the way.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: He leads the humble in justice, and He teaches the humble His way.
All the paths of the LORD are lovingkindness and truth to those who keep His covenant and His testimonies.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: For Your name's sake, O LORD, pardon my iniquity, for it is great.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Who is the man who fears the LORD? He will instruct him in the way he should choose.

words.g: in/with + in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: His soul will abide in prosperity, and his seed will inherit the land.

words.m: preposition + verb hiphil infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: The secret of the LORD is for those who fear Him, and He will make them know His covenant.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The secret of the LORD" (sod YHWH) -- sod means intimate counsel, the inner circle of confidants. In the divine council, sod refers to the heavenly assembly where God reveals His plans (cf. Jer. 23:18, Amos 3:7). Those who fear YHWH are admitted to His confidence -- they know His covenant (berito) from the inside.

verses.flow: My eyes are continually toward the LORD, for He will pluck my feet out of the net.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Turn to me and be gracious to me, for I am lonely and afflicted.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The troubles of my heart are enlarged; bring me out of my distresses.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Look upon my affliction and my trouble, and forgive all my sins.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: Look upon my enemies, for they are many, and they hate me with violent hatred.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Guard my soul and deliver me; do not let me be ashamed, for I take refuge in You.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix
verb piel perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Let integrity and uprightness preserve me, for I wait for You.
Redeem Israel, O God, out of all his troubles.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Of David. Vindicate me, O LORD, for I have walked in my integrity, and I have trusted in the LORD without wavering.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb piel imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Examine me, O LORD, and try me; test my mind and my heart.

words.m: conjunction + verb hithpael sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: For Your lovingkindness is before my eyes, and I have walked in Your truth.
I do not sit with deceitful men, nor will I go with pretenders.
I hate the assembly of evildoers, and I will not sit with the wicked.

words.m: conjunction + verb poel cohortative 1st common sing

verses.flow: I shall wash my hands in innocence, and I will go about Your altar, O LORD,

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb piel infinitive construct

verses.flow: that I may proclaim with the voice of thanksgiving and declare all Your wonders.
O LORD, I love the habitation of Your house and the place where Your glory dwells.

words.g: blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: Do not take my soul away along with sinners, nor my life with men of bloodshed,

words.g: to fill or be full of

verses.flow: in whose hands is a wicked scheme, and whose right hand is full of bribes.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: But as for me, I shall walk in my integrity; redeem me and be gracious to me.
My foot stands on a level place; in the assemblies I shall bless the LORD.
Of David. The LORD is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? The LORD is the defense of my life; whom shall I dread?
verses.note: HEBREW: "The LORD is my light" (YHWH 'ori) -- light as a metaphor for salvation, guidance, and life itself (cf. Ps. 36:9, Isa. 60:19-20). "The defense of my life" (ma'oz chayyay) -- ma'oz means fortress, stronghold. With YHWH as both light (offensive, illuminating) and fortress (defensive, protecting), David asks the rhetorical question: whom shall I fear? The answer is no one.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal participle active + suffix
conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: When evildoers came upon me to devour my flesh, my adversaries and my enemies, they stumbled and fell.
Though a host encamp against me, my heart will not fear; though war arise against me, in spite of this I shall be confident.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb piel infinitive construct

verses.flow: One thing I have asked from the LORD, that I shall seek: that I may dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life, to behold the beauty of the LORD and to meditate in His temple.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "One thing I have asked from the LORD" -- the singular focus of David's life: to dwell in God's presence. "To behold the beauty of the LORD" (lachazot beno'am-YHWH) -- no'am means pleasantness, loveliness, delight. "To meditate in His temple" (ulvaqqer behekhalo) -- baqqer means to inquire, examine, consider. David seeks not merely to attend the temple but to contemplate God's nature within it. This is the beating heart of biblical worship: desiring God Himself above all His gifts.

When the most fundamental human bonds fail, God becomes the ultimate parent. This is not a statement about David's actual parents but a hypothetical extreme: even if everyone abandoned him, God would not.

verses.flow: Save Your people and bless Your inheritance; be their shepherd also, and carry them forever.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "Save Your people and bless Your inheritance; be their shepherd also, and carry them forever" -- the psalm ends with royal intercession. The king prays for the people as their shepherd. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: Christ is the King who saves, blesses, shepherds, and carries His people forever (Isa. 40:11, John 10:27-28).

verses.flow: A Psalm of David. Ascribe to the LORD, O sons of God, ascribe to the LORD glory and strength.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sons of God" (benei 'elim) -- literally "sons of the mighty" or "sons of gods." This is divine council language (cf. Ps. 82:1, 89:6, Job 1:6, 2:1, 38:7). The psalm opens in the heavenly court, calling upon the celestial beings to ascribe glory to YHWH. This is not polytheism but a hierarchical worldview: YHWH is the supreme God, and the heavenly beings worship Him. "Ascribe" (havu) is repeated three times in vv.1-2 -- give, render, attribute -- what belongs to YHWH must be acknowledged.

verses.flow: Ascribe to the LORD the glory due His name; worship the LORD in the splendor of holiness.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The splendor of holiness" (hadrat-qodesh) -- hadrah means honor, majesty, adornment. Worship of YHWH requires holy attire, both literally (priestly vestments) and metaphorically (moral purity). The heavenly beings approach YHWH in the beauty appropriate to His holiness.

verses.flow: The voice of the LORD is over the waters; the God of glory thunders, the LORD is over many waters.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The voice of the LORD" (qol YHWH) appears seven times in this psalm (vv.3, 4a, 4b, 5, 7, 8, 9) -- seven, the number of divine completeness. The voice of YHWH is not mere sound but creative and destructive power (Gen. 1:3, "God said"; Heb. 1:3, "upholding all things by the word of His power"). The theophany moves from waters (v.3) to cedars (v.5) to wilderness (v.8) to forests (v.9) -- all of creation responds to God's voice.

verses.flow: The voice of the LORD is powerful; the voice of the LORD is majestic.

verses.flow: The LORD sat as King at the flood; yes, the LORD sits as King forever.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The LORD sat as King at the flood" (YHWH lammabbul yashav) -- mabbul is used exclusively for the Genesis flood (Gen. 6-9) elsewhere in the OT. YHWH was enthroned as King even over the deluge, the most catastrophic event in human history. If He ruled over the flood, He rules over everything. "The LORD sits as King forever" -- His sovereignty is neither temporary nor threatened.

verses.flow: The LORD will give strength to His people; the LORD will bless His people with peace.
A Psalm; a Song at the Dedication of the House. Of David.
verses.note: HEBREW: "A Song at the Dedication of the House" (shir-chanukkath habbayith) -- traditionally associated with the dedication of the temple (or David's palace). This psalm was later used at the rededication of the temple under the Maccabees -- the festival of Hanukkah (chanukkah) takes its name from this same root (chanakh = to dedicate).

verses.flow: Sing praise to the LORD, you His godly ones, and give thanks to His holy name.
For His anger is but for a moment, His favor is for a lifetime; weeping may endure for a night, but a shout of joy comes in the morning.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "His anger is but for a moment, His favor is for a lifetime" (ki rega' be'appo chayyim birtsono) -- a profound statement of proportion. God's wrath is momentary (rega' = an instant); His favor (ratson) is lifelong. "Weeping may endure for a night, but a shout of joy comes in the morning" (ba'erev yalin bekhi, welaboqer rinnah) -- the night of suffering is temporary; the morning of joy is decisive. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: The cross was the night of weeping; Easter morning was the shout of joy.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Will the dust praise You? Will it declare Your faithfulness?" -- the same argument as Ps. 6:5 and 88:10-12. The dead cannot worship, so preserving life is preserving praise. This is not a denial of afterlife but an appeal to God's desire for worship as a motivation for deliverance.

verses.flow: You have turned for me my mourning into dancing; You have loosed my sackcloth and girded me with gladness,
verses.note: HEBREW: "You have turned for me my mourning into dancing" (hafakhta misped-li lemachol li) -- hafakh means to turn, overturn, transform. "Loosed my sackcloth" (pittachta saqi) -- sackcloth was the garment of mourning and penitence. God removes the garments of grief and dresses His servant in gladness (simchah). This reversal pattern -- mourning to joy, death to life, silence to praise -- is the fundamental rhythm of redemption.

verses.flow: Many, O LORD my God, are the wonders which You have done, and Your thoughts toward us; there is none to compare with You. If I would declare and speak of them, they would be too numerous to count.
Sacrifice and meal offering You have not desired; my ears You have opened. Burnt offering and sin offering You have not required.
verses.note: "My ears You have opened" — Heb. oznayim karita li, lit. "ears You have dug for me." The LXX rendered this "a body You have prepared for me," which Hebrews 10:5 follows. The Hebrew emphasizes obedient hearing; the Greek emphasizes incarnation — both point to Christ's willing submission to the Father.

verses.flow: Then I said, "Behold, I come; in the scroll of the book it is written of me.
verses.note: "In the scroll of the book it is written of me" — Hebrews 10:7 applies this to Christ. The "scroll" (megillah) may refer to the Torah's requirements for the king (Deut 17:18-20) or to God's eternal purpose.

verses.flow: I delight to do Your will, O my God; Your law is within my heart."
verses.note: "I delight to do Your will" — the heart of messianic obedience. Heb 10:5-10 interprets vv. 6-8 as Christ's words at the incarnation, establishing the superiority of His obedience over animal sacrifice.

verses.flow: I have proclaimed glad tidings of righteousness in the great assembly; behold, I will not restrain my lips, O LORD, You know.

verses.note: "What I did not steal, I then have to restore" — the innocent one pays for crimes he did not commit. Typologically applied to Christ's vicarious atonement.

verses.flow: May his name endure forever; may his name increase as long as the sun shines; and let men bless themselves by him; let all nations call him blessed.
verses.note: "Let men bless themselves by him; let all nations call him blessed" — echoing the Abrahamic promise: "in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed" (Gen 12:3). The Davidic king is the channel of Abrahamic blessing to the world.

verses.flow: And blessed be His glorious name forever; and may the whole earth be filled with His glory. Amen and Amen.
verses.note: "May the whole earth be filled with His glory. Amen and Amen" — the doxology closing Book II, and the grandest vision of the Psalter so far: God's glory filling all creation (cf. Num 14:21; Hab 2:14; Isa 6:3).

verses.flow: The prayers of David the son of Jesse are ended.
verses.note: "The prayers of David the son of Jesse are ended" — an editorial colophon marking the end of the primary Davidic collection (though Davidic psalms appear later). This verse is part of the canonical text.

verses.flow: A Psalm of Asaph. Surely God is good to Israel, to those who are pure in heart.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Surely God is good to Israel" ('akh tov leyisra'el Elohim) — the word 'akh ("surely, only") sets up the psalm's tension. Asaph's conclusion comes first: God IS good. But the journey to that conclusion nearly destroyed him (vv.2-16). "Pure in heart" (barey levav) echoes the Beatitude of Matt. 5:8.

words.m: verb qal passive perfect 3rd fem sing
verb qal passive perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: But as for me, my feet had almost stumbled; my steps had nearly slipped.
For I was envious of the arrogant when I saw the prosperity of the wicked.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The prosperity of the wicked" (shalom resha'im) — shalom here means completeness, wholeness, material well-being. The wicked appear to have the very peace that should belong to the righteous. This is the ancient and persistent problem of theodicy (cf. Job; Jer. 12:1; Hab. 1:13).

verses.flow: For there are no pains in their death, and their body is fat.
They are not in trouble as other men, nor are they plagued like mankind.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: Therefore pride is their necklace; violence covers them as a garment.
Their eye bulges from fatness; the imaginations of their heart run riot.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: They mock and wickedly speak of oppression; they speak from on high.
They have set their mouth against the heavens, and their tongue parades through the earth.
Therefore his people return here, and waters of abundance are drunk by them.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: And they say, "How does God know? And is there knowledge with the Most High?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "How does God know? And is there knowledge with the Most High?" — the wicked deny divine omniscience. If God does not see, there is no accountability. This practical atheism (not denying God's existence but denying His awareness) is the foundation of all sin (cf. Ps. 10:11; 94:7; Ezek. 8:12).

verses.flow: Behold, these are the wicked; and always at ease, they have increased in wealth.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Surely in vain I have kept my heart pure and washed my hands in innocence;

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: for I have been stricken all day long and chastened every morning.
If I had said, "I will speak thus," behold, I would have betrayed the generation of Your children.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: When I pondered to understand this, it was troublesome in my sight,
until I came into the sanctuary of God; then I perceived their end.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Until I came into the sanctuary of God" ('ad 'avo 'el miqdeshey-El) — the turning point of the psalm. The problem of the wicked's prosperity is not solved by philosophy but by worship. In God's presence, Asaph gains an eternal perspective that earthly observation could not provide. This is the psalmic answer to the problem of theodicy.

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Surely You set them in slippery places; You cast them down to destruction.
How they are destroyed in a moment! They are utterly swept away by sudden terrors!
Like a dream when one awakes, O Lord, when arousing Yourself, You will despise their image.
When my heart was embittered and I was pierced within,
then I was senseless and ignorant; I was like a beast before You.
Nevertheless I am continually with You; You have taken hold of my right hand.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nevertheless I am continually with You" (wa'ani tamid 'immakh) — despite his near-fall, Asaph was never actually separated from God. This is one of the most profound statements of divine preservation in the Psalter. God held his right hand even when he did not know it.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: With Your counsel You will guide me, and afterward receive me to glory.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "And afterward receive me to glory" (we'achar kavod tiqqacheni) — the verb laqach (take, receive) is the same used for Enoch's translation (Gen. 5:24) and Elijah's ascent (2 Kings 2:3, 9). Asaph glimpses a destiny beyond death — afterlife hope emerging within the Psalter.

verses.flow: Whom have I in heaven but You? And besides You, I desire nothing on earth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Whom have I in heaven but You?" (mi li vashamayim) — Asaph's ultimate conclusion transcends the original question. The prosperity of the wicked becomes irrelevant. God Himself — not His gifts — is the psalmist's portion (cheleq, v.26). This is the summit of Old Testament devotion.

verses.flow: My flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the rock of my heart and my portion forever.
For behold, those who are far from You will perish; You have destroyed all those who are unfaithful to You.
But as for me, the nearness of God is my good; I have made the Lord GOD my refuge, that I may tell of all Your works.

verses.flow: A Maskil of Asaph. O God, why have You rejected us forever? Why does Your anger smoke against the sheep of Your pasture?
verses.note: HEBREW: Maskil — a wisdom or instruction psalm. "Why have You rejected us forever?" (lamah zanachta lanetsach) — the strongest communal lament language. The destruction of the Temple (vv.3-8) is described in vivid, devastating detail.

verses.flow: Turn Your footsteps toward the perpetual ruins; the enemy has damaged everything within the sanctuary.
Your adversaries have roared in the midst of Your meeting place; they have set up their own signs for signs.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: And now all its carved work they smash with hatchet and hammers.
They have set Your sanctuary on fire; they have defiled the dwelling place of Your name to the ground.
They said in their heart, "Let us completely subdue them." They have burned all the meeting places of God in the land.
We do not see our signs; there is no longer any prophet, nor is there any among us who knows how long.
How long, O God, will the adversary revile, and the enemy spurn Your name forever?
Why do You withdraw Your hand, even Your right hand? From within Your bosom, destroy them!

words.g: a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: You divided the sea by Your strength; You broke the heads of the sea monsters in the waters.
verses.note: HEBREW: "You divided the sea" ('attah phorrarta veyam) — the Exodus language shifts to creation mythology. "Sea monsters" (tanninim) and "Leviathan" (v.14) represent chaos forces that God subdued at creation and at the Red Sea. This combat myth imagery (sometimes called chaoskampf) affirms God's sovereign power over all forces of chaos and evil.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "You crushed the heads of Leviathan" — Leviathan here represents the multi-headed chaos serpent known throughout ancient Near Eastern mythology (cf. Ugaritic Litanu). The psalmist appeals to God's original victory over chaos as grounds for a new act of deliverance. If God conquered Leviathan, He can surely defeat Israel's earthly enemies.

A Psalm of Asaph. God takes His stand in the congregation of God; He judges in the midst of the gods.
verses.note: DIVINE COUNCIL: "God (Elohim) takes His stand in the congregation of God (El); He judges in the midst of the gods (elohim)" — this is the foundational divine council text. The Hebrew 'adat-El means "the assembly/congregation of El (God)." The term elohim in the final clause refers to divine beings — members of the heavenly court. This is not polytheism but cosmic hierarchy: YHWH is the supreme God who presides over a council of subordinate divine beings (cf. 1 Kings 22:19-22; Job 1:6; 2:1; Isa. 6:1-8; Dan. 7:9-10). The Septuagint renders the final elohim as "gods" (theoi). This assembly is a judicial setting — God rises to render verdict against the corrupt members of His own council.

verses.flow: "How long will you judge unjustly and show partiality to the wicked? Selah.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God speaks directly to the elohim. Their charge: they have judged unjustly and shown partiality to the wicked. These divine beings were apparently given authority over the nations (cf. Deut. 32:8-9 in the Dead Sea Scrolls reading: "He set the boundaries of the peoples according to the number of the sons of God"). They have failed in their stewardship.

verses.flow: Vindicate the weak and fatherless; do justice to the afflicted and destitute.
Rescue the weak and needy; deliver them out of the hand of the wicked.
They do not know nor do they understand; they walk about in darkness; all the foundations of the earth are shaken.
verses.note: HEBREW: "All the foundations of the earth are shaken" (yimmotu kol mosdey 'arets) — the failure of the divine council members to govern justly has cosmic consequences. Injustice destabilizes creation itself. The "foundations of the earth" represent the moral order that undergirds physical reality.

verses.flow: I said, "You are gods, and all of you are sons of the Most High.
verses.note: DIVINE COUNCIL / CHRIST TYPOLOGY: "I said, 'You are gods (elohim), and all of you are sons of the Most High (benei 'Elyon)'" — God Himself declared these beings to be elohim and benei Elyon. This is the verse Jesus quotes in John 10:34-35 when accused of blasphemy for claiming to be God's Son. Jesus' argument: if Scripture itself calls these lesser beings "gods," how much more appropriate is the title for the one whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world? The verse confirms the existence of a class of divine beings beneath YHWH who hold the title elohim, while simultaneously affirming that they are subordinate and accountable to the Most High.

verses.flow: Nevertheless you will die like men and fall like one of the princes."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Nevertheless you will die like men (ke'adam temuton) and fall like one of the princes (kesarim)" — the sentence of mortality is pronounced on immortal beings. These elohim, who were entrusted with governing nations, will be stripped of their divine status and die like humans. This is a demotion and death sentence. The "princes" (sarim) may refer to angelic princes over nations (cf. Dan. 10:13, 20-21 where Michael is the "prince" of Israel). Their judgment anticipates the eschatological judgment of all spiritual powers (1 Cor. 6:3; Rev. 20:10).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Arise, O God, judge the earth!" (qumah Elohim shophta ha'arets) — the psalm concludes with a plea for God Himself to take over the judging that the corrupt elohim have botched. Since the subordinate divine beings failed, YHWH must assume direct rule over all nations. This is the eschatological hope: God will reclaim all authority. "For it is You who possesses all the nations" (ki 'attah tinchal bekhol-haggoyim) — all nations belong to God, not to the fallen council members. The psalm moves from divine council corruption to divine reclamation of sovereignty.

The intense longing for God's presence (v.3, "my soul longed and even yearned") uses three Hebrew words for desire: kasaph (longed), kalah (yearned/pined), and ranan (sings for joy). Body and soul together reach toward God.

verses.flow: For a day in Your courts is better than a thousand. I would rather stand at the threshold of the house of my God than dwell in the tents of wickedness.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "A day in Your courts is better than a thousand" — the Hebrew is literally "I have chosen to stand at the threshold (histopheph) of the house of my God rather than dwell (dur) in the tents of wickedness." Histopheph means to stand at the doorstep, the lowest possible position. The psalmist would rather be a doorkeeper in God's house than a prince in the world. This radical preference for God's presence over worldly comfort is the heart of biblical piety.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Lovingkindness and truth have met together; righteousness and peace have kissed each other" (chesed we'emet nifgashu, tsedeq weshalom nashaquu) — one of the most celebrated verses in the Psalter. Four divine attributes are personified and reconciled: chesed (covenant love), emet (truth/faithfulness), tsedeq (righteousness), and shalom (peace). The tension between justice (which condemns) and mercy (which forgives) is resolved in their embrace. Christian theology sees this fulfilled at the cross, where God's righteousness and mercy meet in Christ's atoning death (Rom. 3:25-26).

verses.note: HEBREW: Attributed to Heman the Ezrahite (cf. 1 Kings 4:31; 1 Chron. 6:33). This is the darkest psalm in the Psalter — it begins in anguish and ends in darkness (v.19). Some scholars see in it a foreshadowing of Christ's experience of God-forsakenness on the cross (Matt. 27:46), where the sinless one entered total darkness on behalf of sinners.

verses.note: DAVIDIC COVENANT: "I have made a covenant with My chosen; I have sworn to David My servant" (karatti verit livechiri nishba'ti leDavid 'avdi) — this is the Psalm's summary of 2 Sam. 7:8-16, the Davidic covenant. The key promises: eternal seed (zera', v.5), eternal throne (kisse', v.5). This covenant is unconditional — even if David's sons sin (vv.31-33), God will not revoke it (vv.34-35). The NT identifies Jesus as the ultimate fulfillment of this covenant (Luke 1:32-33; Acts 2:29-36; Rev. 22:16).

verses.note: DIVINE COUNCIL: "The heavens will praise Your wonders, O LORD; Your faithfulness also in the assembly of the holy ones (qehal qedoshim)" — the "holy ones" (qedoshim) are the members of the divine council. Verse 7 makes this explicit: "who in the skies (shachaq) is comparable to the LORD? Who among the sons of the mighty (benei elim) is like the LORD?" Benei elim literally means "sons of gods" or "sons of the mighty ones" — these are divine beings, the same class as the elohim of Ps. 82 and the benei ha'elohim of Job 1:6.

verses.flow: For who in the skies is comparable to the LORD? Who among the sons of the mighty is like the LORD,
verses.note: DIVINE COUNCIL: "Who among the sons of the mighty (benei elim) is like the LORD" — this is a rhetorical question expecting the answer "no one." The divine council members are real, powerful beings, but YHWH is incomparably greater. Verse 8 continues: "a God greatly feared in the council of the holy ones (sod qedoshim)" — sod means "council, secret assembly, intimate circle." God presides over this council with absolute authority. The other members fear Him because He is "awesome above all those who are around Him."

verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: "He will cry to Me, 'You are my Father, my God, and the rock of my salvation'" — the Davidic king calls God "Father" ('avi). This father-son relationship between God and the king (cf. 2 Sam. 7:14; Ps. 2:7) is fulfilled supremely in Jesus, who is both David's son and God's eternal Son (Matt. 22:41-46; Heb. 1:5).

verses.note: HEBREW: "I also shall make him My firstborn (bekhor)" — bekhor does not necessarily mean first in birth order but first in rank and privilege. Applied to David's line, it means the Davidic king holds preeminence among all kings. Applied to Christ, it finds its fullest expression in Col. 1:15 ("the firstborn of all creation") and Rev. 1:5 ("the firstborn of the dead").

verses.flow: But You have cast off and rejected; You have been full of wrath against Your anointed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The psalm's dramatic turn. After 38 verses of covenant celebration, vv.39-52 are a devastating lament. God seems to have broken His own covenant — the king is defeated, the crown profaned, the walls broken. This tension between promise and reality is one of the deepest in Scripture. It is resolved only in the resurrection of the Davidic Messiah (Acts 2:25-36; 13:32-37).

verses.note: HEBREW: "A Prayer of Moses, the man of God" (tephillah leMosheh 'ish ha'Elohim) — the only psalm attributed to Moses, making it potentially the oldest composition in the Psalter. "Lord, You have been our dwelling place" (ma'on) — God Himself is Israel's home, not any physical location. This was especially meaningful for a generation that wandered forty years without a permanent dwelling.

verses.flow: Before the mountains were born or You gave birth to the earth and the world, even from everlasting to everlasting, You are God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "From everlasting to everlasting, You are God" (me'olam 'ad 'olam 'attah El) — one of the most direct statements of God's eternality in Scripture. Before creation, God was. After all things pass, God will be. The verb "gave birth" (techolel, from chul, to writhe in labor) applies creation language to the earth and world — God brought them forth.

verses.note: HEBREW: "A thousand years in Your sight are like yesterday" ('eleph shanim be'eynekha keyom 'etmol) — the radical disproportion between divine and human time. Peter quotes this principle in 2 Pet. 3:8 to explain the apparent delay of Christ's return. God's patience operates on a timescale incomprehensible to mortals.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Teach us to number our days" (limnot yameynu ken hoda') — the practical conclusion of the psalm. If life is brief (vv.3-11) and God is eternal (vv.1-2), then wisdom consists of living in light of that proportion. "A heart of wisdom" (levav chokhmah) comes from reckoning honestly with mortality.
